title: Global Content Delivery
content:
Amazon CloudFront (CDN) Integration In section 5.3, we uploaded our static website (Frontend) to Amazon S3 but selected “Block all public access” to ensure maximum security. Therefore, users cannot access the website directly via the S3 link.
In this section, we will create an Amazon CloudFront Distribution (Content Delivery Network). CloudFront will act as the single, secure entry point allowing users worldwide to access the Frontend quickly and safely.
1. Initialize CloudFront Distribution Step 1: Initialize CloudFront Distribution
Navigate to Amazon CloudFront → select Create distribution. Distribution name: Enter zopee-ecommerce. Distribution type: Select Single website or app. Description: Leave blank or enter a description for the Distribution. Route 53 managed domain: If the domain zopee.xyz is already managed by Route 53 in the same AWS account, enter zopee.xyz and click Check domain. If the domain is managed by another DNS provider, leave this blank and configure the domain after the Distribution is created. Click Next to proceed to the Specify origin step. Step 2: Specify Origin
Origin type: Select Elastic Load Balancer. Origin: Select the Application Load Balancer (my-app-alb) created in the previous step. Origin path: Leave blank. Step 3: Enable Security
Enable security protections: Select Enable security protections. Web Application Firewall (WAF): Keep the default configuration recommended by CloudFront. Use monitor mode: Do not select. Rate limiting: Enable. SQL protections: Enable. Protection against Layer 7 DDoS attacks: Enable. Click Next to proceed to the Get TLS certificate step. Origin settings: Select Use recommended origin settings. Cache settings: Select Use recommended cache settings. Click Next to proceed to the next step. Step 4: Configure TLS Certificate
Under Available certificates, select the ACM certificate created for the domain zopee.xyz. Confirm the certificate includes the following domains: zopee.xyz *.zopee.xyz (Wildcard) Ensure the certificate was issued by AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) in the US East (N. Virginia) - us-east-1 region. Click Next to proceed to the Review and create step. Click Create distribution. 2. Grant CloudFront access to read the S3 Bucket (Bucket Policy) After clicking Create, if CloudFront displays a prompt requiring you to update the S3 Bucket Policy, click Copy policy, then navigate to Amazon S3 → select your Frontend Bucket → Permissions → Bucket policy → Edit, paste the copied policy, and click Save changes.
If you missed the prompt, you can still retrieve and configure the Bucket Policy by following these steps:
Navigate to Amazon CloudFront → select the newly created Distribution. Switch to the Origins tab and select the Origin pointing to your S3 Bucket - > Edit. Check the Origin access control section: If using Origin Access Control (OAC), CloudFront usually provides a Copy policy or View policy option to copy the Bucket Policy. After updating the Bucket Policy, click Save changes.
If the Bucket Policy already grants access to CloudFront (s3:GetObject), no further action is required.
3. Access your E-commerce Website! Go back to the General tab of your CloudFront Distribution. You will see the Distribution domain name (e.g., d123...cloudfront.net). The status (Last modified) will initially show as Deploying. Be patient and wait about 3-5 minutes. Once deployment is complete, copy that domain name, paste it into your browser, and enjoy your highly secure and blazing-fast E-commerce website!

content:

title: Resource Cleanup
content:
After completing the workshop, cleaning up AWS resources is extremely important to avoid unexpected charges. You must delete resources in the reverse order of creation to prevent dependency errors.
Follow the steps below sequentially:
1. Delete CloudFront Distribution Navigate to CloudFront > Distributions. Select the zopee-ecommerce distribution and click Disable. (This takes about 3-5 minutes). Once the status changes to Disabled, select it again and click Delete. 2. Delete Amazon ElastiCache, CloudWatch and SNS Navigate to Amazon ElastiCache → Valkey caches → Select the created Cache → Actions → Delete. If you created a separate Subnet group, navigate to Subnet groups → Select the subnet group → Delete. Navigate to Amazon CloudWatch → Alarms → Select the created CPU Alarm → Actions → Delete. Navigate to Amazon SNS → Topics → Select the system-alerts Topic → Delete. 3. Delete Amazon ECS & ECR Navigate to Amazon ECS > Clusters > Select my-app-cluster. Go to the Services tab, select the running Services, and click Delete (Or update desired tasks to 0 before deleting). After the Services are deleted, click the Delete Cluster button in the top right corner. Navigate to Amazon ECR > Repositories, select the Frontend/Backend repositories, and click Delete. 4. Delete Load Balancer & Auto Scaling Group Navigate to EC2 > Load Balancers > Select my-app-alb > Actions > Delete. Go to Target Groups > Select frontend-tg and backend-tg > Delete. Go to Auto Scaling Groups (at the bottom of the left menu) > Select the ECS ASG > Delete. Check Instances to ensure all EC2 instances are Terminated. 5. Delete Cognito & Route 53 (Optional) Navigate to Amazon Cognito > User Pools > Select your User Pool > Click Delete (Confirm by typing the requested text). (If you don’t want to keep the domain) Navigate to Route 53 > Hosted Zones > Select the zopee.xyz zone > Delete. 6. Delete VPC & Security Groups Navigate to VPC > NAT Gateways > Select your NAT Gateway > Click Delete NAT gateway. (Wait 2-3 minutes for it to be fully deleted). Navigate to VPC > Elastic IPs > Select the EIP attached to the NAT Gateway > Actions > Release Elastic IP addresses. Navigate to VPC > Your VPCs > Select e-commerce-vpc > Click Actions > Delete VPC. (This automatically deletes associated Subnets, Route Tables, and Internet Gateway). (If any remain) Navigate to EC2 > Security Groups > Delete alb-sg, ecs-ec2-sg, and redis-sg. 7. Delete DynamoDB & S3 Buckets Navigate to DynamoDB > Tables > Select your table > Click Delete. Navigate to Amazon S3 > Buckets. To delete a bucket, you must empty it first: Select the Bucket > Click Empty (Type permanently delete to confirm). Once empty, select the Bucket again > Click Delete.

title: Domain & SSL (Route 53, ACM)
content:
Setting up Domain Name and SSL Certificate Before diving into deploying the Backend and Application Load Balancer (ALB), the system requires you to have an SSL Certificate (ACM) ready to encrypt HTTPS connections. This ensures application security and fulfills Amazon Cognito’s requirements. To provision an SSL certificate, you must own a custom domain and manage it via Amazon Route 53.
This guide will show you how to create a Hosted Zone in Route 53 to manage your domain and request an SSL certificate. (Since AWS Free Tier accounts do not support direct domain registration on Route 53, you must register a domain from a third-party provider like Hostinger, Namecheap, etc., before starting).
Step 1: Create a Hosted Zone in Amazon Route 53 Navigate to the Amazon Route 53 console. In the left navigation menu, select Hosted zones. Click Create hosted zone. Domain name: Enter the custom domain you purchased from the third-party provider (e.g., zopee.xyz). Type: Keep it as Public hosted zone. Click Create hosted zone. Once created, you will see 4 name server addresses in the Value/Route traffic to column of the NS (Name server) record. Go back to your third-party domain registrar’s dashboard, find the DNS / Name Servers (NS) configuration, and replace the existing nameservers with the 4 NS values provided by Route 53. Wait a short time for the DNS to propagate. Step 2: Request an SSL Certificate from AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) Once your domain is active in the Hosted Zone, you need to create an SSL/TLS certificate. CRITICAL: Because our architecture will use CloudFront (in section 5.7), you MUST switch your AWS Region to US East (N. Virginia) - us-east-1 before requesting the ACM certificate! (CloudFront only accepts ACM certificates from us-east-1). Ensure the AWS Region dropdown at the top-right corner is set to US East (N. Virginia) - us-east-1. Navigate to the AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) console. Click Request a certificate. Select Request a public certificate, click Next. In the Fully qualified domain name section, enter the following 2 names: zopee.xyz (Replace with your purchased domain) Click Add another name to this certificate *.zopee.xyz (The wildcard covers all subdomains) Validation method: Select DNS validation - recommended. Key algorithm: Leave as RSA 2048. Click Request. Step 3: Validate the SSL Certificate (DNS Validation) The newly requested certificate will initially have a Pending validation status.
Click on the Certificate ID to view its details. Under the Domains section, you will see CNAME records that need to be added to your DNS. Since your domain is managed by Route 53, simply click the Create records in Route 53 button. A prompt will appear listing the CNAME records. Click Create records. Wait for about 2 to 5 minutes. Route 53 will automatically propagate the DNS records, and your certificate status in ACM will turn green and show as Issued.
